points.headline: US lifts export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5, with strings attached.
points.body: The Commerce Department withdrew the emergency curbs it imposed weeks earlier, letting Anthropic restore global access to its most powerful models. In exchange the company added new safety testing and is removing a covert steganographic system it had used to detect Chinese competitors copying its models.

points.headline: Anthropic ships Claude Sonnet 5, a cheaper near-flagship ahead of its IPO.
points.body: Sonnet 5 delivers performance close to Opus 4.8 at mid-tier pricing, aimed at cost-conscious developers building agents and coding tools. It launched the same day on AWS Bedrock as Anthropic races toward a blockbuster public offering.

points.headline: Google pushes fast, cheap generative media with Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash.
points.body: Google released Nano Banana 2 Lite (Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite Image), its fastest and cheapest image model, generating pictures in seconds. Alongside it, Gemini Omni Flash reached the API, letting enterprises produce and revise video through conversation.

points.headline: AI agents and browsers keep getting weaponized.
points.body: Researchers showed Claude Opus 4.7 could be steered to breach a festival ticketing platform and issue free tickets, red teamers turned Claude Desktop into a "double agent," and a prompt-injection trick ("2+2=5") bypassed AI browser guardrails. DeepSeek was also coaxed into producing working in-browser ransomware, while a new site, Flare, lets users report AI misbehavior.

points.headline: Cloudflare forces AI crawlers to pay, reigniting the fight over the web's economics.
points.body: From September 15, Cloudflare will block AI bots by default on many publisher sites unless companies separate search crawling from AI training and agents, pressuring them to pay for content. Commentators warn AI search could hollow out the open web without new quality signals and revenue models.

points.headline: The AI buildout squeezes memory and reshapes the hardware market.
points.body: Surging demand for AI servers is driving DRAM prices up so sharply that budget PCs are being priced out, and Qualcomm is proposing to bury compute under the DRAM to beat the memory wall. Meanwhile Meta and SpaceX are moving to resell excess AI compute, and neocloud Together AI raised $800M at an $8.3B valuation.
